employer NZME — Deputy Political Editor — Press Gallery Wellington source
Key facts
Joined NZME in 2018 as sole sports reporter at Northern Advocate in Whangarei; progressed through Covid/crime reporting to Deputy Political Editor in the Press Gallery source
Background

Adam Pearse is the Deputy Political Editor for the NZ Herald, based in the Parliamentary Press Gallery in Wellington. He joined NZME in 2018 after completing Massey University's postgraduate journalism course, starting as the sole sports reporter at the Northern Advocate in Whangarei. He was nominated for Best Junior Reporter at the 2022 Voyager Media Awards.

employer NZME — Created Sideswipe column (2002-2023) then moved to The Listener source
Background

Ana Samways created and wrote the NZ Herald's daily 'Sideswipe' humour column for over 20 years (2002-2023), one of NZ's longest-running newspaper columns. After the column ended she moved to The Listener's digital edition and launched an independent Substack. She has authored 'Fake', optioned for Paramount+ Australia.

employer NZME — Senior crime and justice journalist since 2008 source
employer Penguin Books NZ — Published true crime author source
Key facts
Hosts 'A Moment In Crime' true crime podcast (iHeart/NZME) and published two books with Penguin NZ including 'Christie' and 'Behind Bars' source
Background

Anna Leask is a senior crime and justice journalist at the NZ Herald, where she has worked since 2008. She hosts the award-winning true crime podcast 'A Moment In Crime' and is a published author with Penguin Books NZ. She has covered NZ's highest-profile criminal cases over 20 years including the March 15 terror attack, Grace Millane murder, and Pike River mine disaster.

employer NZME — Political editor NZ Herald source
Background

Audrey Young is one of New Zealand's most experienced political journalists. She began her career as a sub-editor at the Auckland Sun, and after its closure in 1988 joined the New Zealand Herald as a sub-editor. She rose through the ranks to become the Herald's political editor, a role she held for 18 years (2003-2021), leading the paper's press gallery team in Wellington. In 2021, she transitioned to the role of Senior Political Correspondent, allowing her to focus on in-depth reporting. She was named Political Journalist of the Year at the Voyager Media Awards in 2018, 2020, and 2023. Her 30th anniversary at the Herald was acknowledged in Parliament. She has a particular interest in foreign affairs and Treaty of Waitangi issues, and served for six years as the union representative on the Press Council.

Connections
XBlueskyLinkedInFacebookSubstack
employer The Spinoff — Economics columnist source
Background

Bernard Hickey is a Wellington-based financial journalist, editor, and commentator with over two decades of experience spanning Reuters, the Financial Times Group, and Fairfax Media, working across London, Singapore, and Sydney. He co-founded and edited Interest.co.nz and Newsroom Pro. He is best known for an acknowledged ideological shift over his career: initially a conventional free-market economics commentator, he moved substantially leftward, becoming a vocal critic of New Zealand's housing policies, wealth inequality, and what he characterises as the failure of neoliberal settings to deliver for younger generations. His podcast with The Spinoff, "When the Facts Change" (named after the Keynes aphorism), reflects this evolution. He founded The Kaka, a Substack newsletter covering political economy, housing, climate, and poverty.

employer Stuff — Senior political reporter — Press Gallery (now at Sky News Australia) source

Background

Bridie Witton is a political journalist who served as senior political reporter for Stuff in the Parliamentary Press Gallery. She started at the Rotorua Daily Post and worked for the BBC in London. She is now Sky News Australia's NZ correspondent.

Catrin Owen is a senior journalist at Stuff covering courts, crime, and justice in Auckland. Originally from Wales, she joined Stuff in 2015. She is a regular contributor to BBC Radio Cymru.

employer NZME — Newstalk ZB opinion columnist source
Background

Christopher Marshall Trotter (born 1956) is one of New Zealand's most prominent left-wing political commentators, writing weekly political columns since the early 1980s. He served as Secretary of the Otago Trades Council and was twice elected Industrial Representative to the NZ Council of the Labour Party. He followed Jim Anderton into the NewLabour Party, standing in Dunedin North. He founded the NZ Political Review magazine (1992-2005), authored No Left Turn, and has blogged at Bowalley Road since 2008.

employer NZME — Senior investigative journalist since 2004 source
Background

David Fisher is a senior investigative journalist at the NZ Herald with over 30 years' experience. He has twice been named NZ Reporter of the Year and was a Wolfson Press Fellow at Cambridge. He is known for investigating NZSAS operations in Afghanistan and the true crime podcast 'Chasing Ghosts'.

Connections

employer The Spinoff — Founder and managing editor source
Background

Duncan Greive (born 1979, Auckland) is the founder of The Spinoff, New Zealand's leading independent online media outlet, which he launched in 2014. He studied history at the University of Auckland and worked in marketing before establishing himself as a freelance journalist. In 2015 he co-wrote Dan Carter's bestselling autobiography. He was named Editor of the Year and Media Visionary at the 2017 NZ Marketing Awards. He stepped down as CEO in 2023 but remains founder and chair. He hosts The Fold, a media-focused podcast. His wife Nicola is a lawyer at the Serious Fraud Office.

Georgina Campbell
NZ Herald · Wellington local government
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer NZME — Wellington senior journalist covering local government (2016-2025) source
employer Hutt City Council — Left journalism for local government role in 2025 — poacher turned gamekeeper source
Background

Georgina Campbell was a Wellington-based senior journalist for NZME (NZ Herald and Newstalk ZB) for nine years, covering local government, transport, and seismic resilience. She wrote the 'Capital Letter' column on Wellington affairs and produced the award-winning 'Speaking Secrets' podcast on NZ's MeToo movement. She left journalism in early 2025 to work in local government.

Guyon Espiner
RNZ · Investigative
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer RNZ — Public broadcaster funded by NZ Government source
spouse Emma Wehipeihana — Former personal assistant to Labour MP Darren Hughes (married 2012 separated 2023) source
political Darren Hughes — Labour MP — Espiner's wife Emma Wehipeihana was his personal assistant source
Background

Guyon Espiner (born 31 December 1970, Christchurch) is an investigative journalist at Radio New Zealand with over 30 years in print, television, and radio. After a decade in newspapers, including as political editor of the Sunday Star-Times, he joined TVNZ in 2003, becoming political editor in 2006 and anchoring Q+A, Agenda, and Breakfast. He moved to TV3's 60 Minutes in 2011, then joined RNZ in 2014 as co-host of Morning Report. Since 2019 he has produced podcasts (The Ninth Floor, The Service, Red Line) and documentaries. He authored The Drinking Game. Espiner married Emma Wehipeihana in 2012; they separated in 2023. Wehipeihana had previously worked as a personal assistant to Labour MP Darren Hughes, who served as MC at their wedding.

employer Stuff — Deputy political reporter source
Key facts
Left journalism to work in public relations in London for several years before returning as deputy political reporter at The Post in September 2025; runs the 'Museum Street' politics newsletter source
Background

Henry Cooke is a political journalist at Stuff/The Post. He studied international relations, political science, and media studies at Victoria University of Wellington. He joined Stuff through a summer internship, moving to the press gallery in 2017. He rose to chief political reporter and deputy political editor. He left journalism to work in public relations in London before returning as deputy political reporter at The Post in September 2025. He runs the 'Museum Street' politics newsletter on Substack.

Jamie Ensor
NZ Herald · Politics
Connections

employer NZME — Chief Political Reporter — Press Gallery Wellington source

Background

Jamie Ensor is the Chief Political Reporter for the NZ Herald, based in the Parliamentary Press Gallery. He joined from Newshub in 2024 following its closure. He was a finalist for Political Journalist of the Year at the 2025 Voyager Media Awards.

employer RNZ — Public broadcaster funded by NZ Government source
Background

Jane Patterson is a senior journalist at Radio New Zealand who joined in May 2000. She spent most of her career in the Parliamentary Press Gallery, where she is a life member, including eight years as RNZ's political editor. She was appointed Director of Editorial Quality and Training at RNZ. Her awards include Best Coverage of a News Event for Election 2017 at the 2018 Voyager Awards.

spouse Andrew Ketels — Former ACT Party Chief of Staff (5 years) and National Party ministerial adviser source
employer Stuff — Political editor source
Background

Jenna Lynch is a senior New Zealand political journalist. She began her career at the Waikato Times before joining TV3/MediaWorks in 2014, becoming Newshub's political editor in 2022 after Tova O'Brien's departure. She won Political Journalist of the Year at the 2022 Voyager Media Awards. Following Newshub's closure in July 2024, she moved to Stuff. Lynch's spouse is Andrew Ketels, who served as ACT Party chief of staff for five years and previously worked as a National Party ministerial adviser.

employer RNZ — Political journalist source
Background

Jo Moir is the political editor at Radio New Zealand, a role she took up in early 2024. She completed her postgraduate journalism diploma at Massey University in 2006. She worked at the Wairarapa Times-Age, Taranaki Daily News, and the Dominion Post before joining Stuff's press gallery team in 2015 and then RNZ in 2018. She subsequently served as political editor of Newsroom from 2021 before returning to RNZ. She has reported on five prime ministers and covered three general elections.

Karanama Ruru
Stuff · Te ao Maori and politics
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer Stuff — Te ao Maori and politics reporter since 2022 source
Background

Karanama Ruru is a Stuff reporter covering te ao Maori, politics, health, and culture. He affiliates to Ngati Raukawa and Ngati Maniapoto. He entered journalism via 95bFM student radio and joined Stuff permanently in 2022. His work has been cited in Waitangi Tribunal proceedings.

Kerre Woodham
Newstalk ZB · Current affairs
Connections
X
employer NZME — Newstalk ZB broadcaster (publishes as Kerre McIvor) source
Background

Kerre Woodham (born c. 1964) is a veteran New Zealand journalist, radio host, author and columnist. She studied journalism at Wellington Polytechnic and began her career at Radio New Zealand before moving into television as a reporter on Fair Go (1986-1990). She hosted Ready Steady Cook (1998-2005) and presented Heartland (1994-1997). An author of several books including Short Fat Chick to Marathon Runner, she is one of New Zealand's most recognisable broadcasters with over 35 years in media. She hosts Kerre Woodham Mornings (9am-12pm weekdays) on Newstalk ZB.

employer NZME — Senior diversity journalist 2005-2024 (left Dec 2024) source
employer Chow Luck Club — Co-founder and editor — food and culture digital publication source
Key facts
Received death threats from extremists and a NZ politician for exposing alleged bribery and an imam preaching jihad; broke exclusive MH370 coverage source
Background

Lincoln Tan was the NZ Herald's senior diversity journalist from 2005 to December 2024. Originally from Singapore, he received death threats for exposing alleged bribery and an imam preaching jihad. He co-founded food/culture publication Chow Luck Club in 2021.

Topic Profile
Politics 100%
Connections
employer NZME — NZ Herald columnist and Newstalk ZB panellist source
employer RNZ — Regular political panellist on RNZ Nine to Noon and Morning Report source
political National Party — Paid political consultant for National Party while simultaneously serving as NZ Herald columnist and RNZ panellist source
political ACT Party — Paid political consultant for ACT Party while simultaneously serving as public commentator source
Key facts
Simultaneously NZ Herald columnist and paid political consultant for both National Party and ACT Party over many years; also a regular panellist on RNZ; was press secretary to PM Jim Bolger in the 1990s — this dual role as paid partisan operative and ostensibly neutral commentator is a documented conflict of interest source
Background

Matthew Hooton is a New Zealand PR consultant, lobbyist, and centre-right political commentator. He founded Exceltium in 2005, providing government relations and PR to major clients. He is known for his NZ Herald column and as a panellist on RNZ's political discussions. His dual role as commentator and lobbyist has drawn persistent conflict-of-interest criticism. In 2020, he was hired as a National Party consultant after helping engineer Todd Muller's leadership coup but resigned within months. He later settled a defamation claim brought by former National MP Steven Joyce.

Michael Neilson
NZ Herald · Social issues
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer NZME — Social issues reporter source
Background

Michael Neilson is a New Zealand journalist from Christchurch who studied politics at the University of Otago before completing a journalism diploma at AUT in 2015. He joined the NZ Herald in 2018, covering social issues, the environment, and Maori affairs, later becoming a senior political reporter in the press gallery. He has since transitioned to freelance journalism and photography and is currently based in Indonesia.

Connections
employer NZME — Newstalk ZB broadcaster source
Background

Ryan Bridge (born November 1987, Kapiti Coast) is a New Zealand broadcaster and journalist. He began as a political reporter in the parliamentary press gallery for RNZ, later becoming TV3's Beijing correspondent and co-host of AM on Three. When Newshub closed in 2024, he moved to NZME. He publicly came out as gay in 2021. In May 2025, he launched Herald NOW, a live-streamed morning news show. He hosts Early Edition (5-6am weekdays) on Newstalk ZB and Herald NOW on NZME.

employer TVNZ — Co-host of TVNZ Breakfast (from April 2026) source
Background

Tova O'Brien (born c. 1982-83) is one of New Zealand's highest-profile political journalists. Born in Papua New Guinea, she began at TV3/MediaWorks in 2007, rising to Newshub's political editor in 2018. She won Political Journalist of the Year (2019). She briefly moved to Today FM in 2022 before it folded, then joined Stuff. In December 2025, TVNZ announced O'Brien would join Breakfast as co-host from April 2026.

All years · 10 articles
Topic Profile
Politics 60%Transport 20%Health 10%Environment 10%
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer Stuff Limited — Formerly Fairfax NZ source
political Peter Dunne — United Future leader and Revenue Minister — Textgate 2013 (86 emails in 13 days; Dunne resigned; Vance's phone records and parliamentary swipe card data were improperly accessed) source
Key facts
'Textgate' (2013): exchanged 86 emails over 13 days with Revenue Minister Peter Dunne about GCSB leak inquiry; Dunne resigned; a subsequent inquiry improperly accessed Vance's phone records AND parliamentary swipe card movement data — a major NZ privacy scandal source
Background

Andrea Vance is a Northern Irish political journalist currently serving as a senior journalist at Stuff in Wellington. She grew up near Belfast, where her father edited three local newspapers. She worked for seven years as an investigative reporter at the British tabloid News of the World. When the phone hacking scandal broke, Vance was in the Glasgow newsroom and has denied any personal involvement, stating "no one had any idea it was going on." She subsequently worked as night news editor at The Scotsman before emigrating to New Zealand with her Kiwi husband. She became a prominent press gallery journalist, working for Fairfax/Stuff and later 1News. In 2013, she was at the centre of the Peter Dunne email scandal, in which minister Dunne resigned after refusing to release 86 email exchanges with Vance regarding a leaked GCSB report. Her phone records and parliamentary swipe-card data were improperly provided to the inquiry without her knowledge, prompting a major privacy breach complaint. She was named Political Journalist of the Year in 2025.

Anna Whyte
Stuff · Politics
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer Stuff — Senior political journalist — Press Gallery source
employer TVNZ — Former Senior Digital Political Producer at 1News source
Background

Anna Whyte is a senior political journalist at Stuff / The Post based in the Parliamentary Press Gallery. She previously worked at TVNZ/1News as Senior Digital Political Producer. She is from Motueka and holds a PGDip Journalism from Massey University.

Barry Soper
Newstalk ZB · Parliamentary politics
Connections
XLinkedIn
spouse Heather du Plessis-Allan — Broadcaster Newstalk ZB source
employer NZME — Parent company of Newstalk ZB and NZ Herald source
Background

Barry Soper (born c. 1951, Gore) is the longest-serving member of New Zealand's Parliamentary Press Gallery, having joined in 1980. After briefly attending the Royal New Zealand Police College in 1969, he entered journalism at The Southland Times, progressing through the Mataura Ensign, Otago Daily Times, New Zealand Truth, and Waikato Times. He joined the press gallery working for APN before moving to Newstalk ZB, where he has served as political editor and senior political correspondent. He has covered eleven prime ministers, both Fijian coups (1987 and 2000), and Nelson Mandela's inauguration. He was named Individual Radio Journalist of the Year in 2001. In 1992, he was approached to stand as a Labour Party candidate in the Wellington Central by-election but declined. He married journalist and broadcaster Heather du Plessis-Allan in 2009. He was named unfavourably in Jacinda Ardern's 2025 memoir.

Key facts
Retired in early 2026 after 36 years at the NZ Herald; described by Steve Braunias in The Listener as a 'legend of the trade'; covered Auckland through four mayors and the Super City creation source
Background

Bernard Orsman was the NZ Herald's Auckland/Super City reporter for 36 years, having joined the paper in 1990. He covered Auckland local government since 1998 through multiple mayoral terms and the creation of the Auckland Super City. He previously spent six years in the Parliamentary Press Gallery. Described as a 'legend of the trade', he retired in early 2026.

employer NZME — Newstalk ZB commentator source
Background

Bruce Cotterill is a New Zealand business leader, professional director, columnist and author. A five-time CEO, he led Colliers NZ and Australia, Canterbury International sportswear, and Yellow Pages Group. He is the author of The Best Leaders Don't Shout and hosts the podcast Leaders Getting Coffee. He writes a regular business and political commentary column for the NZ Herald and Newstalk ZB.

employer NZME — Parent company of NZ Herald source
employer NZ Police — Chief Media and Communications Adviser (from May 2025 — revolving door from NZ Herald Political Editor) source
Key facts
Left NZ Herald in May 2025 after almost two decades as Political Editor to become Chief Media and Communications Adviser to the NZ Police Commissioner source
Background

Claire Trevett served as the NZ Herald's political editor for several years, becoming one of the most recognised press gallery journalists in New Zealand. Based in Wellington, she was known for her sharp political analysis, witty writing style, and coverage of multiple election cycles and changes of government. Trevett left the Herald in May 2025 after almost two decades as Political Editor to become Chief Media and Communications Adviser for New Zealand Police.

Derek Cheng
NZ Herald · Political journalism
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer NZME — Political journalist NZ Herald source
Background

Derek Cheng is a senior political journalist at the New Zealand Herald. He graduated with a journalism diploma from Massey University in 2004 and joined the Herald shortly after, where he has worked for two decades. He has served multiple stints in the parliamentary press gallery and held the role of deputy political editor. His political reporting career spans the Helen Clark, John Key, and Jacinda Ardern eras. He is known for evidence-based political analysis. Outside journalism, Cheng is an accomplished mountaineer and adventure writer.

Background

Edward Gay is a reporter at Stuff covering crime, courts, and justice in Auckland. He has previously worked at RNZ, NZ Herald, and APNZ.

Glenn McConnell
Stuff · Politics and te ao Maori
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer Stuff — Political reporter — Pou Tiaki team since 2016 source
Background

Glenn McConnell is a Wellington-based political reporter for Stuff's Pou Tiaki team since 2016. He has whakapapa to Te Atiawa and Ngati Tama. He was an INMA 30 Under 30 honouree in 2022 and created the award-winning podcast 'He'll Be Right'. His work has appeared in The Guardian, Sydney Morning Herald, and RNZ.

Background

Hamish McNeilly is Stuff's senior reporter in Dunedin with over 15 years' experience. He won Voyager Media Awards Regional Reporter of the Year in both 2019 and 2020. He previously worked at the Otago Daily Times and runs 'The Mish' Substack newsletter.

Isaac Davison is a senior reporter at the NZ Herald covering health and Auckland issues. He joined the Herald in 2008 and spent six years in the Parliamentary Press Gallery covering politics. Key facts
Left NZ Herald in April 2025 after 14 years as science reporter to become Deputy Editor at The Conversation New Zealand source
Background

Jamie Morton is a science and environment journalist with 20+ years' experience. He spent 14 years at the NZ Herald (2011-2025) before joining The Conversation New Zealand as Deputy Editor. He has reported from Antarctica and global climate summits. He has been named Science Reporter of the Year four times (2013, 2016, 2022, 2023).

employer RNZ — Public broadcaster funded by NZ Government source
political Jacinda Ardern — Former NZ Prime Minister — personal friend; named in Ardern's 2025 memoir; interviewed Ardern on RNZ on memoir release source
Key facts
Personal friend of Jacinda Ardern; named in Ardern's 2025 memoir; conducted the first major interview with Ardern following the memoir's release on RNZ Afternoons source
Background

Jesse Mulligan is a New Zealand broadcaster, food critic, and former comedian. He hosts RNZ National's Afternoons since 2015. His television career includes co-hosting TVNZ's Seven Sharp (2013-2014) and co-hosting The Project on Three (2017-2023). He is the dining-out editor for Viva magazine and writes the I Ate Auckland newsletter on Substack.

John Campbell
RNZ · Morning Report
Connections
X
employer RNZ — Public broadcaster funded by NZ Government source
spouse Emma Patterson — Documentary editor source
employer TVNZ — Chief Correspondent at TVNZ/1News source
Background

John Campbell (born 10 February 1964) is one of New Zealand's most recognised broadcast journalists. He began at Radio New Zealand as a business reporter in 1989. He moved to TV3 in 1991, co-anchoring 3 News (1998-2005). He fronted Campbell Live (2005-2015), whose cancellation prompted significant public debate. He returned to RNZ in 2016 to host Checkpoint, then joined TVNZ in 2018. In December 2025 he was announced as co-host of RNZ's Morning Report from April 2026. He is married to documentary editor Emma Patterson.

Kate Hawkesby
Newstalk ZB · Current affairs
Connections
X
employer NZME — Newstalk ZB broadcaster source
Background

Kate Hawkesby (born 1 April 1973) is a New Zealand broadcaster, columnist and former television presenter. She holds a Political Studies degree and worked at London's Daily Express before returning to NZ. At TVNZ (1995-2007), she became the youngest person to present a One News at Six bulletin and co-anchored Breakfast with Mike Hosking in 2002. She hosted Newstalk ZB's Early Edition (5-6am weekdays) from 2017 until departing in November 2023. She is married to Mike Hosking. She is now an NZ Herald columnist and regular Friday panellist on The Mike Hosking Breakfast.

employer NZME — South Island Head of News source
Key facts
Won Crime & Justice Reporter of the Year AND Regional Journalist of the Year at 2021 Voyager Media Awards; ghost-wrote Sunday Times No. 1 bestseller 'The Forgotten Highlander' source
Background

Kurt Bayer is the NZ Herald's South Island Head of News, based in Christchurch. Before joining the Herald in 2011, he spent a decade at a UK news agency and ghost-wrote the Sunday Times No. 1 bestseller 'The Forgotten Highlander'. He won Crime & Justice Reporter of the Year and Regional Journalist of the Year at the 2021 Voyager Media Awards.

Lisa Owen
Newsroom · Political interviews
Connections
X
Background

Lisa Owen is one of New Zealand's most experienced political broadcasters. She spent 14 years at TVNZ before moving to TV3/MediaWorks, where she hosted The Nation, the network's flagship weekend political programme. She hosted the Drive show on RadioLive before being appointed presenter of RNZ's Checkpoint in late 2018, replacing John Campbell. Owen won Best Talk Presenter at the 2021 NZ Radio Awards. She is widely regarded as one of New Zealand's sharpest political interviewers.

XBlueskyLinkedInFacebook
employer Stuff — Inequality and democracy columnist source
Background

Max Rashbrooke (born 1980, Wellington) is a New Zealand journalist, author, and researcher best known for his work on economic inequality. He studied at Victoria University of Wellington. From 2003 to 2010 he worked as a political journalist in London, including freelancing for The Guardian. He has authored four books on inequality, including Inequality: A New Zealand Crisis (2013). He has undertaken research contracts for organisations including the Green Party. In 2024, he co-founded the Institute for Democratic and Economic Analysis (IDEA).

Foreign Affairs 20%Media 20%Economy 20%Sport 10%Politics 10%Transport 10%

employer NZME — Host of Mike Hosking Breakfast on Newstalk ZB source
Background

Mike Hosking (born 1965, Dunedin) is New Zealand's most prominent radio broadcaster. He left school at 16, starting as an advertising copywriter before getting his break on Wellington's Radio Windy. He hosted Radio New Zealand's Tonight Show and Morning Report, then joined Newstalk ZB in 2008 as weekday Breakfast host — a role he still holds. On television, he co-presented TVNZ's Breakfast (1997-2005) and Seven Sharp (2014-2017). He writes a regular opinion column for the NZ Herald. Hosking is an open supporter of the centre-right National Party and is frequently critical of Labour-led governments. His career has drawn multiple successful Broadcasting Standards Authority complaints. He is married to fellow Newstalk ZB broadcaster Kate Hawkesby. A landmark 2003 privacy case involving Hosking established "the Hosking principles" in NZ common law.

Anna Fifield
Stuff · Editor-at-large
Connections
XBlueskyLinkedIn
employer Stuff Limited — Formerly Fairfax NZ source
Background

Anna Fifield (born 1976, Hastings) is a New Zealand journalist with an extensive international career. She studied English at Victoria University of Wellington and completed a postgraduate journalism diploma at the University of Canterbury. At 25, she moved to London and joined the Financial Times, where she spent 13 years as a foreign correspondent covering the Middle East, Korea, and US politics. She joined The Washington Post in 2014 as Tokyo bureau chief, later becoming Beijing bureau chief. She won the 2018 Shorenstein Journalism Award from Stanford and authored "The Great Successor" (2019), a book on Kim Jong Un translated into 24 languages. She was also a Nieman Fellow at Harvard. Returning to New Zealand in 2020, she edited The Dominion Post/Stuff's Wellington newsroom until December 2022. She returned to the Washington Post as Asia-Pacific editor but was laid off in February 2026. In March 2026, she joined The Spinoff to write the World Bulletin newsletter.

Anne Gibson
NZ Herald · Property
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer NZME — Property Editor since 2000 source
industry REINZ — Three-time REINZ Property Media Awards supreme winner — deep property industry ties source
Background

Anne Gibson is the NZ Herald's long-serving Property Editor, covering real estate since 1985 and joining the Herald in 2000. She founded NBR's property section in the 1980s and was the first female sub-editor at the Christchurch Star. She has won the REINZ Property Media Awards supreme award three times and the Canon Media Awards Business Feature Writer Award (2016).

Belinda Feek
NZ Herald · Crime and courts
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer NZME — Open Justice reporter based in Hamilton source
Background

Belinda Feek is an Open Justice multimedia journalist at the NZ Herald based in Hamilton, with 20+ years in journalism. She is of Ngati Kahungunu descent. She has covered major events including the Kaikoura earthquake and Whakaari/White Island eruption. She started her career in Oamaru before working in Whanganui and moving to Hamilton in 2007.

Blair Ensor
Stuff · Investigative
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer Stuff — Senior journalist — investigative and national reporting source
Background

Blair Ensor is a senior investigative journalist at Stuff with over a decade of experience. He has won multiple Voyager Media Awards for crime and justice reporting. He led the 'Faces of Innocents' child homicide investigation and 'The Homicide Report'. He was among the first journalists at Al Noor mosque during the 2019 Christchurch attack.

Bryce Edwards
Stuff · Political analysis
Connections
XLinkedInFacebookSubstack
employer Stuff — Political analyst source
political Integrity Institute — Director and trustee - collapsed 2025 amid accusations of funding pre-determined journalism targeting right-leaning organisations source
political Alliance Party / Workers Party — Alliance electoral liaison executive assistant (2001); Workers Party member; spoke at Marxism 2007 conference source
political Gama Foundation — Received $487,000 in philanthropic funding for Democracy Project research source
Background

Bryce Edwards is a political commentator and academic based at Victoria University of Wellington, where he is a Research Associate in the Institute of Governance and Policy Studies and Director of the Democracy Project. He holds a PhD in political sociology (2004) and previously lectured in Political Studies at Otago University. Edwards has a well-documented history on the political left. He helped found the Canterbury University Progressive Left Club in 1993 and was a contact for the Canterbury University Marxist Society in 1995. He worked in the Alliance Party's electoral liaison unit in 2001 as an executive assistant. In 2007, he was identified as a member of the Workers Party and spoke at the Marxism 2007 conference. Despite this left-wing background, he has built a mainstream media career as a political analyst, writing regular columns for the NZ Herald, The Guardian, and other outlets. In 2025, his Integrity Institute collapsed amid accusations of funding journalists to produce pre-determined investigative stories targeting right-leaning organisations, with critics calling it the very 'dirty politics' it claimed to oppose.

Chris Keall
NZ Herald · Technology and business
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer NZME — Technology Editor and Senior Business Writer source
Background

Chris Keall is the Technology Editor and Senior Business Writer at the NZ Herald with 20+ years covering tech and business. He previously edited NZ PC World, Internet Magazine, and was technology editor at NBR. He has won two Qantas Media Awards for feature writing and authored 'Download: The Internet Made Easy' (HarperCollins).

David Farrar
NZ Herald · Political commentary
Connections
XLinkedInFacebook
employer Kiwiblog — Political commentator and pollster source
Background

David Farrar (born 1967) is a New Zealand political blogger, pollster, and activist with deep ties to the National Party. He worked in Parliament for four National leaders, including in the Prime Minister's Office under Jim Bolger (1996-97) and Jenny Shipley (1997-99). He was national secretary of the Young Nationals. In 2003 he founded Kiwiblog, which became New Zealand's most widely read political blog. In 2004 he founded Curia Market Research, which serves as the National Party's internal pollster. In 2013 he co-founded the New Zealand Taxpayers' Union with Jordan Williams. He writes a regular column for the NZ Herald. The 2009 NZ Listener Power List ranked him the fourth most powerful person in New Zealand media.

Duncan Garner
Newshub · Political commentary
Connections
X
political Guyon Espiner — RNZ journalist — served as best man at Espiner's 2012 wedding to Emma Wehipeihana source
Background

Duncan Garner (born 8 March 1974) is a New Zealand broadcaster and journalist. He joined TVNV in the mid-1990s, then moved to TV3 in 2003. He won the 2004 Television Political News Reporter award for exposing a golden handshake to Labour MP John Tamihere. He served as Newshub's political editor before hosting The AM Show (2017-2021). He briefly presented on Today FM (2022-2023). He has attracted controversy over race: a 2017 column was found by the Press Council to constitute "gratuitous racism."

Key facts
Dual role as NZME Head of Business/Herald columnist AND Managing Director of NZ Inc (business intelligence) while serving on NZ China Council and NZ US Council advisory boards — multiple potential conflicts when covering trade and foreign policy source
Broke stories leading to the Winebox Inquiry while at NBR and the Sydney Morning Herald; ONZM recipient for services to journalism source
Runs the annual 'Mood of the Boardroom' CEO survey for the Herald — the survey's corporate sponsors and participants overlap with subjects of her columns source
Background

Fran O'Sullivan is Head of Business at NZME and a prominent business-political columnist for the NZ Herald since 1997. She previously edited the National Business Review and served as Herald Assistant Editor (2001-2005). She is Managing Director of NZ Inc, co-chairs the China Business Summit, and sits on the NZ China Council Advisory Board. She was made an ONZM for services to journalism and has been twice named Journalist of the Year.

Gordon Campbell is the Political Editor at Scoop and editor of Werewolf, a monthly digital journal of longform journalism on politics, art, and culture. He was previously a highly regarded feature writer and rock music critic at the NZ Listener. He has published 3-4 political commentary columns per week since 2008 and is a regular attendee at the Prime Minister's weekly press conference.

spouse Barry Soper — Political journalist Newstalk ZB source
employer NZME — Parent company of Newstalk ZB and NZ Herald source
Background

Heather du Plessis-Allan (born 1984, South Africa) migrated to New Zealand aged 12 and is the host of the Newstalk ZB Drive show, a role she has held since 2019. She previously worked at TV3, Radio Live, and TVNZ. She is married to veteran political journalist Barry Soper. In 2015, while at TV3's Story, du Plessis-Allan sparked controversy by purchasing a firearm online using false details to expose weaknesses in New Zealand's Arms Act. Police raided her home and investigated but ultimately did not lay charges. She won Broadcaster of the Year at the 2025 NZ Radio and Podcast Awards.

employer TVNZ — Host of Q+A political interview programme source
Key facts
Host of TVNZ Q+A since 2014; his December 2022 interview with Willie Jackson was widely described as a 'trainwreck' after Jackson repeatedly challenged Tame's framing source
Background

Jack Tame (born 4 March 1987, Christchurch) is one of New Zealand's most prominent broadcast journalists. He served as TVNZ's United States correspondent (2012-2017) before co-hosting Breakfast (2017-2019) and then taking over Q+A in 2019, where he has built a reputation for rigorous interviewing. In late 2025 he was named weekend presenter of 1News at Six. He also presents on Newstalk ZB. He married fellow TVNZ journalist Mava Moayyed in 2023.

Jane Clifton
Newsroom · Political commentary
Connections
X
employer Newsroom — Political columnist source
spouse Trevor Mallard — Labour MP and Speaker of the House (married Dec 2014); served as NZ Ambassador to Ireland 2022-2024 source
political Murray McCully — National MP and Foreign Minister — Clifton was in a relationship with McCully for approximately 20 years before her marriage to Mallard source
Background

Jane Clifton is one of New Zealand's most experienced political commentators. She began journalism at age 16 in 1978 and spent roughly 20 years in Parliament's press gallery. She is best known for her political column in the NZ Listener, which she wrote for approximately 30 years. She married Labour MP (later Speaker of the House) Trevor Mallard in December 2014. She had previously been in a long relationship with National MP Murray McCully. Her writing is noted for its cross-partisan independence.

employer NZME — Parent company of NZ Herald source
Key facts
Moved from NZ Herald to Newstalk ZB Political Editor then to TVNZ 1News Business Correspondent (replacing Katie Bradford) source
Background

Jason Walls is a New Zealand political and business journalist currently serving as business correspondent at TVNZ/1News. He holds degrees in economics and journalism from AUT. He began at NBR and Interest.co.nz before joining the NZ Herald's press gallery team in 2018, rising to political editor. He moved to Newstalk ZB in 2021, becoming political editor in 2023. Walls served as Chair of the Parliamentary Press Gallery. His wife, Jacinta Gulasekharam, co-founded Dignity NZ, a social enterprise combating period poverty.

Jessica Mutch McKay
1News · Political editor
Connections
XLinkedInFacebook
spouse Iain McKay — Diplomatic Protection Squad officer (provided security for PMs Key English and Ardern) source
employer ANZ Bank — Head of Government Relations and Corporate Responsibility (from early 2024 — revolving door from TVNZ Political Editor) source
Background

Jessica Mutch McKay is a former New Zealand television political journalist who served as TVNZ's 1News political editor. She joined Sky News in 2006 before moving to TVNZ in 2008. She hosted Q+A before becoming political editor in 2018, leading coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2023 general election. In early 2024, she departed TVNZ to become Head of Government Relations and Corporate Responsibility at ANZ Bank. Her husband, Iain McKay, is a Diplomatic Protection Service bodyguard who served on the personal protection detail of Prime Minister Ardern.

Julia Gabel
NZ Herald · Data journalism and politics
Connections
XLinkedIn
employer NZME — Multimedia journalist — data journalism and political reporting source
Background

Julia Gabel is a multimedia data journalist at the NZ Herald, where she has worked since 2020. She holds a Postgraduate Diploma in Journalism from AUT and lived in Beijing for two years before entering journalism. She won the 2025 Voyager Media Award for Best Innovation in Digital Storytelling for 'Whenua: Our Land, Our History'. She also contributes to Kahu, the Herald's Maori news platform.

Katie Bradford

Background

Katie Bradford is a veteran New Zealand political and business journalist with over 20 years' experience. She is the daughter of former Green Party MP and activist Sue Bradford and activist Jim Bradford. She spent more than a decade at TVNZ, working primarily as 1News business correspondent. In April 2025, Bradford left TVNZ to join Infrastructure New Zealand as strategy and engagement manager.

Background

Liam Dann is Business Editor at Large at the NZ Herald, having led the Business Herald through the Global Financial Crisis as editor (2007-2015). He has been a financial journalist for over 25 years. He hosts the 'Money Talks' podcast and authored the bestselling 'BBQ Economics: How money works and why it matters' (Penguin, 2024). He previously worked at Marc Rich Investments in London.

Background

Mani Dunlop (Ngapuhi, Scottish descent) is a Wellington-born journalist who began her career as an RNZ intern in 2011. She became the first Maori journalist to host a regular weekday programme on RNZ when she took over the Midday Report in 2020. She departed RNZ in March 2023 after being passed over for the Morning Report co-host role. She subsequently joined Whakaata Maori as a senior political editor at Te Ao Maori News.

Michael Daly is a senior journalist at Stuff based in Wellington covering climate, environment, and weather. He has an extensive body of work on climate science and government environmental policy.

employer NZME — Investigative reporter — health (departed ~early 2025) source
Background

Nicholas Jones is an investigative reporter who worked at the NZ Herald specialising in health investigations. He won multiple Voyager Media Awards. He departed the Herald in early 2025.

Connections

employer NZME — Senior writer at NZ Herald (from 2018) and The Listener source
Background

Simon Wilson is an award-winning senior writer at the NZ Herald covering politics, climate, transport, housing, and urban design. He was formerly editor of Metro magazine and has worked in magazines since the mid-1980s. He authored 'Homeground' (2022) about the Auckland City Mission and the novel 'The Age of Light' (1994). He is the first non-fiction writer to win the Nigel Cox Unity Books Award.

employer The Spinoff — Editor-at-large source
Background

Toby Manhire is a New Zealand journalist, editor, and the son of Bill Manhire, New Zealand's inaugural Poet Laureate. He edited student magazine Salient in 1997 before spending over a decade in London, where he worked at The Guardian, including as editor of its comment pages. He is editor-at-large at The Spinoff and host of Gone By Lunchtime, a popular politics podcast alongside Annabelle Lee-Mather and Ben Thomas.

How We Score

Complete transparency in our methodology — every step is open source

-1.0 -0.6 -0.2 0 +0.2 +0.6 +1.0
Left Centre-Left Centre Centre-Right Right
1

Article Discovery

We crawl sitemaps from NZ's major news outlets to build a comprehensive index of every published article. For each URL, we extract the author name using outlet-specific strategies — APIs, structured data, or streaming HTML.

Outlets: NZ Herald, Stuff, RNZ, The Spinoff, Newstalk ZB, 1News, Newsroom
2

Text Extraction

Full article text is extracted using trafilatura, an open-source library designed for web content extraction. The extracted text is cleaned of navigation, ads, and boilerplate before scoring.

Minimum threshold: Articles under 200 characters are excluded as likely stubs or fragments
3

AI Scoring

Each article is scored by Claude Sonnet 4.5 (Anthropic) on a scale from -1.0 (hard left) to +1.0 (hard right). The AI evaluates five dimensions of bias for each article and applies the "Cui Bono" principle — asking who benefits from the story being published.

Model: claude-sonnet-4-5  |  Prompt version: v3-nuance
4

Aggregation

Individual article scores are aggregated into five buckets for each journalist. The spectrum position shown on each card is the median score across all their scored articles. Confidence ratings (low/medium/high) reflect the number of articles scored.

Buckets: Left (<-0.6) · Centre-Left (-0.6 to -0.2) · Centre (-0.2 to 0.2) · Centre-Right (0.2 to 0.6) · Right (>0.6)

The Five Dimensions of Bias

Every article is evaluated across these five dimensions, each scored independently:

◀
Story Selection

The editorial choice of what to cover. Publishing a story about one party's scandal while ignoring the other's is itself a signal — even before a word is written.

■
Framing

How is the topic presented? Who is positioned as protagonist or antagonist? The frame shapes reader perception before they process the facts.

▶
Source Selection

Which voices are quoted? Are opposing views included? Over-representing one side's experts or spokespeople creates an imbalanced narrative.

▲
Language

Loaded words like "slammed", "controversial", "radical", or "common sense" inject editorial judgement into ostensibly neutral reporting.

▼
Omission

What relevant context is missing? Profiling a politician's controversial views while omitting their actual policy record is bias by absence.

Worked Examples

Two fictional articles showing how the same scoring framework produces different results:

Left-leaning example
"Government's housing selloff leaves thousands in limbo"
Fictional Author · Score: -0.45 · Bucket: Centre-Left
AI Reasoning

The article frames government housing policy as a "selloff" — loaded language implying reckless disposal rather than neutral "reform" or "restructuring". Sources are predominantly tenant advocacy groups and opposition MPs, with only a single paragraph quoting the Housing Minister. The cui bono test: this story benefits parties advocating for more state housing, which skews left. The omission of waiting list reduction data under the policy further tilts the framing.

Story Selection-0.3
Framing-0.5
Source Selection-0.5
Language-0.6
Omission-0.4
Right-leaning example
"Taxpayers foot the bill as council's pet project blows budget"
Fictional Author · Score: +0.40 · Bucket: Centre-Right
AI Reasoning

The article frames a public infrastructure project as wasteful government spending — "pet project" and "foot the bill" are loaded phrases that prime the reader to see public investment negatively. Sources are dominated by a taxpayer advocacy group and business owners, with the council's justification confined to a brief quote. The cui bono test: this story benefits parties advocating for reduced government spending, which skews right. The omission of the project's employment and economic impact data tilts the narrative further.

Story Selection+0.3
Framing+0.5
Source Selection+0.4
Language+0.5
Omission+0.3

The Exact Prompt

In the spirit of full transparency, this is the exact prompt sent to the AI for every article. Nothing is hidden. View source on GitHub →

Click to expand the full scoring prompt 
You are a political bias analyst for New Zealand media. Score the following
news article on a scale from -1.0 (hard left) to +1.0 (hard right).

Consider these dimensions:
- FRAMING: How is the topic presented? Who is the protagonist/antagonist?
- SOURCE SELECTION: Which politicians, experts, or voices are quoted?
- LANGUAGE: Is loaded or emotive language used?
- TOPIC EMPHASIS: What aspects are highlighted vs downplayed?
- OMISSION: What relevant context or perspectives are missing?

CRITICAL — ask "CUI BONO?" (who benefits from this story being published?):
- An article exposing a right-wing party's failures BENEFITS THE LEFT
- An article exposing a left-wing party's problems BENEFITS THE RIGHT
- The editorial choice of what to write about is itself a signal of lean
- "Both sides quoted" does NOT mean neutral

NZ political context:
- Centre-left = Labour, Greens
- Centre-right = National, ACT
- Centre/populist = NZ First (has governed with both Labour and National)
- Te Pati Maori = indigenous rights focus; often aligns left on economic/social policy

Returns: score (-1.0 to 1.0), confidence, reasoning, and per-dimension scores.

Limitations & Caveats

  • AI is not infallible. Large language models can misinterpret sarcasm, cultural context, or NZ-specific political nuance. Individual article scores should be taken with a grain of salt — the aggregate pattern across hundreds of articles is what matters.
  • Bias is multidimensional. A single left-right spectrum is a simplification. Journalists may be progressive on social issues but conservative on economic policy. We use five sub-dimensions to partially address this.
  • Article availability varies. Not all articles are accessible for scoring. Some may be unavailable, removed, or behind access restrictions, which can affect representation.
  • Correlation is not intent. A journalist whose work scores left-leaning may be accurately reporting on a left-leaning government's policies. Context matters.
  • This is a starting point, not a verdict. Bias exists to surface patterns and encourage critical reading — not to label journalists as biased.
Bias · Open source at GitHub
